perturbation.original_text: Recipient shall: (a) only disclose the Confidential Information to those employees and contractors with a need to know; provided that Recipient binds those employees and contractors to terms at least as restrictive as those stated in this Agreement (or, with respect to professional advisors, advises them of its obligations with respect to the Confidential Information); (b) not disclose any Confidential Information to any third party, without Discloser’s prior written consent
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient shall: (a) endeavor to disclose the Confidential Information only to those employees and contractors with a possible need to know; Recipient should inform those employees and contractors of its obligations with respect to the Confidential Information; (b) attempt not to disclose any Confidential Information to any third party, without Discloser’s consent
perturbation.explanation: The original text uses definitive language like 'shall' and 'binds,' creating a clear obligation. The modified text uses softer language like 'endeavor,' 'should inform,' and 'attempt not to disclose,' which makes the obligation less strict and more discretionary, which in turn creates ambiguity and makes it less enforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: The California Trade Secrets Act requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. Changing 'shall' to weaker terms like 'endeavor' and 'attempt' lowers the standard of care required to protect confidential information, potentially falling short of the 'reasonable efforts' standard required by the Act.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to protect confidential information, potentially contradicting the requirement for 'reasonable efforts' under the California Trade Secrets Act. The original text's 'shall' and 'binds' are replaced with the less stringent 'endeavor' and 'attempt,' which are less enforceable. Snippet 1 defines 'misappropriation' as including the disclosure or use of a trade secret without consent by a person who 'knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was...acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use'. By weakening the language, the recipient's duty to maintain secrecy is less clear, creating ambiguity that could lead to a finding that the recipient did not have a duty, thus potentially undermining the protections of the Act. The modified language makes the obligation discretionary rather than mandatory.

perturbation.original_text: All copies shall bear all proprietary markings contained on or in the originals. Upon the request of Discloser, Recipient shall, at Recipient’s option, either (a) return such materials to Discloser, or (b) certify the destruction thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: All copies should ideally bear proprietary markings similar to those contained on or in the originals. Upon the request of Discloser, Recipient may, at Recipient’s convenience, either (a) return such materials to Discloser, or (b) consider certifying the destruction thereof.
perturbation.explanation: The original text sets a clear requirement for all copies to have proprietary markings and obligates the recipient to return or destroy materials upon request. The modified text introduces ambiguity by using 'should ideally,' 'similar to,' 'may,' and 'consider,' making the obligations discretionary rather than mandatory.
perturbation.law_explanation: The California Uniform Trade Secrets Act allows for injunctive relief to prevent misappropriation of trade secrets. By weakening the requirement to properly mark and return/destroy the documents, the altered text reduces the protection and could lead to easier misappropriation. The original law requires proper control to prevent this; the modification weakens this safeguard.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.2. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.2. (a) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (b) If the court determines that it would be unreasonable to prohibit future use, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time the use could have been prohibited. (c) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the protection of trade secrets, creating a contradiction. The original contract language mandates certain actions to protect proprietary information, while the changed language makes these actions discretionary. The Law Explanation states that the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act allows for injunctive relief to prevent misappropriation of trade secrets, and the modified text reduces this protection by weakening the requirements to properly mark, return, or destroy documents. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing California Code, CIV 3426.2, confirms that 'actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined' and 'affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order.' By making the protective actions discretionary, the modified contract undermines the possibility of injunctive relief to protect trade secrets. The changes from 'shall' to 'may' and similar weakening changes remove legal certainty and create a contradiction with the intent of the law.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient hereby acknowledges and agrees that no remedy at law will afford Discloser adequate protection against, or appropriate compensation for, breach of Recipient’s obligations under this Agreement. Accordingly, Recipient agrees that Discloser shall be entitled to specific performance of Recipient’s obligations, as well as such further equitable relief as may be granted by a court of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient acknowledges that remedies at law may not fully protect Discloser against breach of Recipient’s obligations under this Agreement. Accordingly, Recipient suggests that Discloser might be entitled to specific performance of Recipient’s obligations, as well as equitable relief as determined by a court.
perturbation.explanation: The original text clearly states that legal remedies are inadequate and the Discloser is entitled to specific performance. The modified text weakens this by saying remedies 'may not fully protect' and that the Discloser 'might be entitled,' making the availability of specific performance uncertain.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 3384 grants specific performance when monetary damages are inadequate. By using language that the Discloser 'might be entitled' to specific performance and legal remedies 'may not fully protect', the modification creates doubt, whereas the original text acknowledges their right to it. This creates a contradiction as the law intends to make specific performance available when needed, and the text is now ambiguous.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. SPECIFIC AND PREVENTIVE RELIEF [3366 - 3424] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 2. Specific Relief [3375 - 3415] ( Chapter 2 enacted 1872. ) ARTICLE 3. Specific Performance of Obligations [[3384.] - 3395] ( Article 3 enacted 1872. ) [3384.] Section Thirty-three Hundred and Eighty-four. Except as otherwise provided in this Article, the specific performance of an obligation may be compelled. (Amended by Code Amendments 1873-74, Ch. 612.)
perturbation.justification: The original text provides a strong assertion of Discloser's entitlement to specific performance, while the changed text weakens this assertion, suggesting only that the Discloser 'might be entitled' to it. This introduces ambiguity regarding a remedy that the original text presents as assured. According to the Law Explanation, California Civil Code § 3384 favors specific performance when monetary damages are inadequate. The scraped snippet 1, referencing CIV 3384, states that 'the specific performance of an obligation may be compelled'. The modification undermines the enforceability of the specific performance obligation, making it uncertain and thus contradicting the intent of the law to ensure its availability when required.
